title: Rothkäppchen
about: The twenty-sixth tale (KHM 26): the girl in the red velvet cap dawdles on her way to grandmother’s, the wolf reaches the house first, and both are swallowed — then cut free by the huntsman. The 1857 spelling gives the old form Rothkäppchen (Roth-, Thür, gieng).
source: Brüder Grimm, Kinder- und Hausmärchen, Band 1, 7. Auflage (Ausgabe letzter Hand), Dieterich, Göttingen 1857, KHM 26. Text from Wikisource (twice proofread against the original). 1857 spelling preserved exactly.

sentences.t: Es war einmal eine kleine süße Dirne, die hatte jedermann lieb, der sie nur ansah, am allerliebsten aber ihre Großmutter, die wußte gar nicht was sie alles dem Kinde geben sollte.
sentences.l: There was once a little sweet lass, whom everyone loved who but looked at her, most of all, however, her grandmother, who knew not at all what all she should give the child.
sentences.i: Once upon a time there was a dear little girl whom everyone loved who only set eyes on her, but most of all her grandmother, who did not know what in the world she should give the child.
sentences.n: “Es war einmal” is the fixed fairy-tale opening, ‘once upon a time.’ “die hatte jedermann lieb” — the object “die” (her) stands first for emphasis; “lieb haben” = to love, hold dear. “dem Kinde” is dative with the old -e ending (‘to the child’). “Dirne” here keeps its older neutral sense ‘young girl, lass,’ not the later pejorative one. “wußte” is the 1857 spelling of wusste.

sentences.t: Einmal schenkte sie ihm ein Käppchen von rothem Sammet, und weil ihm das so wohl stand, und es nichts anders mehr tragen wollte, hieß es nur das Rothkäppchen.
sentences.l: Once she gave it a little cap of red velvet, and because it became it so well, and it wanted to wear nothing else any more, it was called only Little Red Cap.
sentences.i: One day she gave her a little cap of red velvet, and because it suited her so well, and she would wear nothing else, she was called only Little Red Cap.
sentences.n: The child is grammatically neuter (das Kind), so German keeps saying “ihm” and “es” (‘it’) for her throughout — read them as ‘her.’ “das stand ihm wohl” — stehen + dative here means ‘to suit, become someone.’ “rothem” (and later “roth”) keeps the old -th- (modern rot); “Sammet” is the older form of Samt (‘velvet’). “hieß es” — heißen here is ‘to be called.’

sentences.t: Eines Tages sprach seine Mutter zu ihm „komm, Rothkäppchen, da hast du ein Stück Kuchen und eine Flasche Wein, bring das der Großmutter hinaus; sie ist krank und schwach und wird sich daran laben.
sentences.l: One day its mother spoke to it, “come, Little Red Cap, here you have a piece of cake and a bottle of wine, carry it out to the grandmother; she is sick and weak and will refresh herself with it.
sentences.i: One day her mother said to her, “Come, Little Red Cap, here is a piece of cake and a bottle of wine; take them out to Grandmother — she is sick and weak, and they will do her good.
sentences.n: “Eines Tages” is a genitive of time, ‘one day.’ “bring ... hinaus” (hinausbringen, ‘to carry out’) is separable — the prefix hinaus lands at the clause end. “sich laben an” = to refresh / regale oneself on something (reflexive, + dative “daran”).

sentences.t: Mach dich auf bevor es heiß wird, und wenn du hinaus kommst, so geh hübsch sittsam und lauf nicht vom Weg ab, sonst fällst du und zerbrichst das Glas und die Großmutter hat nichts.
sentences.l: Make yourself up (= set out) before it grows hot, and when you get out there, then go nice and decorously and do not run off the path, else you fall and shatter the glass and the grandmother has nothing.
sentences.i: Set out before it grows hot, and when you get out there, walk nicely and properly, and do not run off the path, or you will fall and break the glass, and Grandmother will have nothing.
sentences.n: “Mach dich auf” — sich aufmachen = to set out, get going (separable, reflexive). “lauf ... ab” (ablaufen von) here ‘run off (the path).’ “hübsch sittsam” = ‘nice and decorously.’ The “wenn ... so” frame (‘when ... then’) runs all through Grimm.

sentences.t: Und wenn du in ihre Stube kommst, so vergiß nicht guten Morgen zu sagen und guck nicht erst in alle Ecken herum.“
sentences.l: And when you come into her room, then forget not to say good morning and do not first go peering around into all the corners.”
sentences.i: And when you come into her room, do not forget to say good morning, and do not go poking about into every corner first.”
sentences.n: “vergiß” is the imperative of vergessen (old ß). “guck ... herum” (herumgucken, ‘to look around’) is separable; gucken is a colloquial ‘to look, peer.’ “erst” here = ‘first (of all), before anything else.’

sentences.t: „Ich will schon alles gut machen“ sagte Rothkäppchen zur Mutter, und gab ihr die Hand darauf.
sentences.l: “I will surely do everything well,” said Little Red Cap to the mother, and gave her the hand on it.
sentences.i: “I will do everything just right,” said Little Red Cap to her mother, and gave her her hand on it.
sentences.n: “schon” here is a reassuring particle, ‘surely, don’t worry.’ “jemandem die Hand darauf geben” = to shake hands on it, give one’s word.

sentences.t: Die Großmutter aber wohnte draußen im Wald, eine halbe Stunde vom Dorf.
sentences.l: The grandmother, however, dwelt outside in the wood, half an hour from the village.
sentences.i: But the grandmother lived out in the forest, half an hour from the village.
sentences.n: Note the position of “aber”: it sits after the first element (‘the grandmother, however’) rather than at the front — German very often places aber, doch and nun this way.

sentences.t: Wie nun Rothkäppchen in den Wald kam, begegnete ihm der Wolf.
sentences.l: As now Little Red Cap came into the wood, the wolf met it.
sentences.i: Now when Little Red Cap came into the forest, the wolf met her.
sentences.n: “Wie” here means ‘when, as’ (temporal), not ‘how.’ “begegnen” takes the dative — “begegnete ihm” = met to-it, i.e. met her; you will meet begegnen + dative constantly.

sentences.t: Rothkäppchen aber wußte nicht was das für ein böses Thier war und fürchtete sich nicht vor ihm.
sentences.l: Little Red Cap, however, knew not what kind of an evil beast that was and feared itself not before it.
sentences.i: But Little Red Cap did not know what a wicked beast that was, and was not afraid of him.
sentences.n: “was für ein” = ‘what kind of.’ “Thier” keeps the old -th- (modern Tier). “sich fürchten vor” (+ dative) = to be afraid of.

sentences.t: „Guten Tag, Rothkäppchen,“ sprach er.
sentences.l: “Good day, Little Red Cap,” spoke he.
sentences.i: “Good day, Little Red Cap,” said he.

sentences.t: „Schönen Dank, Wolf.“
sentences.l: “Many thanks, Wolf.”
sentences.n: “Schönen Dank” (‘fine thanks’) is a set phrase of thanks, in the accusative.

sentences.t: „Wo hinaus so früh, Rothkäppchen?“
sentences.l: “Where out to so early, Little Red Cap?”
sentences.i: “Where away so early, Little Red Cap?”
sentences.n: “Wo hinaus?” is an old idiom, ‘to where, whither?’ — ‘where are you off to?’

sentences.t: „Zur Großmutter.“
sentences.l: “To Grandmother’s.”

sentences.t: „Was trägst du unter der Schürze?“
sentences.l: “What carry you under the apron?”
sentences.i: “What are you carrying under your apron?”

sentences.t: „Kuchen und Wein: gestern haben wir gebacken, da soll sich die kranke und schwache Großmutter etwas zu gut thun, und sich damit stärken.“
sentences.l: “Cake and wine: yesterday we baked, so the sick and weak grandmother shall do herself something good, and strengthen herself with it.”
sentences.i: “Cake and wine: yesterday we baked, and now the sick and weak grandmother shall treat herself to something good and fortify herself with it.”
sentences.n: “sich etwas zu gut thun” is an idiom, ‘to treat oneself, indulge.’ “thun” is the 1857 spelling of tun (‘to do’); the old -th- likewise gives Thier, Thüre. “da” here = ‘then, so.’ “gebacken” is the past participle of backen (‘to bake’).

sentences.t: „Rothkäppchen, wo wohnt deine Großmutter?“
sentences.l: “Little Red Cap, where dwells your grandmother?”

sentences.t: „Noch eine gute Viertelstunde weiter im Wald, unter den drei großen Eichbäumen, da steht ihr Haus, unten sind die Nußhecken, das wirst du ja wissen“ sagte Rothkäppchen.
sentences.l: “Yet a good quarter-hour further into the wood, under the three great oak trees, there stands her house, below are the nut-hedges, that you surely know,” said Little Red Cap.
sentences.i: “A good quarter-hour further on in the forest, under the three big oak trees — that is where her house stands, with the hazel hedges down below; you must surely know it,” said Little Red Cap.
sentences.n: “ja” here is a particle of appeal, ‘surely, as you know.’ “Eichbäumen” and “Nußhecken” are compounds (Eiche ‘oak’ + Baum, Nuß ‘nut’ + Hecke).

sentences.t: Der Wolf dachte bei sich „das junge zarte Ding, das ist ein fetter Bissen, der wird noch besser schmecken als die Alte: du mußt es listig anfangen, damit du beide erschnappst.“
sentences.l: The wolf thought to himself, “that young tender thing, that is a fat morsel, it will taste even better than the old one: you must set about it cunningly, so that you snatch both.”
sentences.i: The wolf thought to himself, “That tender young thing — that is a plump morsel; she will taste even better than the old one. You must go about it cunningly, so as to snatch them both.”
sentences.n: “bei sich denken” = to think to oneself. “die Alte” = the old woman (an adjective used as a noun). “es listig anfangen” — anfangen here means ‘to set about, go about’ a thing, not ‘to begin.’ “mußt” is the 1857 spelling of musst.

sentences.t: Da gieng er ein Weilchen neben Rothkäppchen her, dann sprach er „Rothkäppchen, sieh einmal die schönen Blumen, die rings umher stehen, warum guckst du dich nicht um?
sentences.l: There he went a little while along beside Little Red Cap, then he spoke, “Little Red Cap, just look at the beautiful flowers that stand all around; why do you not look about you?
sentences.i: So he walked a little while beside Little Red Cap, and then he said, “Little Red Cap, do look at the beautiful flowers standing all around — why don’t you look about you?
sentences.n: “gieng” is the 1857 spelling of ging (‘went’); this -ie- spelling recurs constantly here (gieng, fieng, anfieng). “gieng ... her” (nebenher gehen) — her marks motion alongside. “guckst du dich ... um” (sich umgucken, ‘to look about’) is separable and reflexive. “rings umher” = round about, all around.

sentences.t: ich glaube du hörst gar nicht, wie die Vöglein so lieblich singen?
sentences.l: I believe you do not hear at all how the little birds sing so sweetly?
sentences.i: I do believe you don’t even hear how sweetly the little birds are singing?
sentences.n: “gar nicht” = ‘not at all.’ “Vöglein” is a diminutive of Vogel (‘little birds’).

sentences.t: du gehst ja für dich hin als wenn du zur Schule giengst, und ist so lustig haußen in dem Wald.“
sentences.l: you go along there for yourself as if you were going to school, and it is so merry out here in the wood.”
sentences.i: you just walk straight ahead as if you were on your way to school, and it is so merry out here in the forest.”
sentences.n: “für dich hin gehen” = to go along by oneself, minding nothing else. “als wenn ... giengst” — als wenn (‘as if’) with the subjunctive; “giengst” is the 1857 form of gingst. “haußen” is an old dialect form of draußen (‘outside’). Note the impersonal “und ist so lustig” with the subject ‘es’ dropped.

sentences.t: Rothkäppchen schlug die Augen auf, und als es sah wie die Sonnenstrahlen durch die Bäume hin und her tanzten, und alles voll schöner Blumen stand, dachte es „wenn ich der Großmutter einen frischen Strauß mitbringe, der wird ihr auch Freude machen; es ist so früh am Tag, daß ich doch zu rechter Zeit ankomme,“ lief vom Wege ab in den Wald hinein und suchte Blumen.
sentences.l: Little Red Cap opened her eyes, and when it saw how the sunbeams danced to and fro through the trees, and everything stood full of beautiful flowers, it thought, “if I bring the grandmother a fresh bouquet, that will give her pleasure too; it is so early in the day that I shall arrive in good time after all,” ran off the path into the wood and sought flowers.
sentences.i: Little Red Cap raised her eyes, and when she saw how the sunbeams danced to and fro through the trees, and all was full of lovely flowers, she thought, “If I bring Grandmother a fresh bouquet, that will please her too; it is so early in the day that I shall still get there in good time,” and she ran off the path into the forest and looked for flowers.
sentences.n: “schlug die Augen auf” — die Augen aufschlagen, ‘to open / raise one’s eyes’ (separable auf-). “mitbringe” (mitbringen, ‘to bring along’) is separable. “zu rechter Zeit” = at the proper time; the present “ankomme” stands for the future (‘I shall arrive’). “lief ... ab ... hinein” marks running off the path and in.

sentences.t: Und wenn es eine gebrochen hatte, meinte es weiter hinaus stände eine schönere, und lief darnach, und gerieth immer tiefer in den Wald hinein.
sentences.l: And when it had picked one, it thought further out there stood a fairer one, and ran after it, and got ever deeper into the wood.
sentences.i: And whenever she had picked one, she fancied that further out there stood a fairer one, and she ran after it, and got deeper and deeper into the forest.
sentences.n: “gebrochen” is the past participle of brechen, here ‘to pick, pluck’ a flower. “stände” is subjunctive II of stehen (‘would stand’) in her reported thought. “darnach” is the 1857 spelling of danach (‘after it’). “gerieth” is the 1857 spelling of geriet (geraten, ‘to get / fall into’).

sentences.t: Der Wolf aber gieng geradeswegs nach dem Haus der Großmutter, und klopfte an die Thüre.
sentences.l: The wolf, however, went straightaway to the grandmother’s house, and knocked on the door.
sentences.i: But the wolf went straight to the grandmother’s house and knocked at the door.
sentences.n: “geradeswegs” = straightaway, directly. “Thüre” keeps the old -th- (modern Tür). “klopfte an” (anklopfen, ‘to knock’) is separable.

sentences.t: „Wer ist draußen?“
sentences.l: “Who is outside?”

sentences.t: „Rothkäppchen, das bringt Kuchen und Wein, mach auf.“
sentences.l: “Little Red Cap, who brings cake and wine, open up.”
sentences.i: “Little Red Cap, bringing cake and wine — open the door.”
sentences.n: “das” refers back to Rothkäppchen (neuter), so ‘who / that.’ “mach auf” (aufmachen, ‘to open’) is separable.

sentences.t: „Drück nur auf die Klinke,“ rief die Großmutter, „ich bin zu schwach und kann nicht aufstehen.“
sentences.l: “Just press on the latch,” called the grandmother, “I am too weak and cannot get up.”
sentences.n: “Drück ... auf” — drücken auf, ‘to press on.’ “nur” here = ‘just.’ “aufstehen” (‘to get up, rise’) is separable.

sentences.t: Der Wolf drückte auf die Klinke, die Thüre sprang auf und er gieng, ohne ein Wort zu sprechen, gerade zum Bett der Großmutter und verschluckte sie.
sentences.l: The wolf pressed on the latch, the door sprang open and he went, without speaking a word, straight to the grandmother’s bed and swallowed her.
sentences.i: The wolf pressed on the latch, the door sprang open, and without a word he went straight to the grandmother’s bed and swallowed her down.
sentences.n: “sprang auf” (aufspringen, ‘to spring open’) is separable. “ohne ... zu sprechen” = ‘without speaking.’ “verschluckte” (verschlucken) = swallowed down.

sentences.t: Dann that er ihre Kleider an, setzte ihre Haube auf, legte sich in ihr Bett und zog die Vorhänge vor.
sentences.l: Then he put her clothes on, set her bonnet on, laid himself in her bed and drew the curtains to.
sentences.i: Then he put on her clothes, set her cap on his head, lay down in her bed, and drew the curtains shut.
sentences.n: “that ... an” is the 1857 spelling of tat ... an (anthun / antun, here ‘to put on’ clothes). A string of separable verbs follows: “setzte ... auf” (aufsetzen), “zog ... vor” (vorziehen, ‘to draw shut’). “Haube” = bonnet, cap; “Vorhänge” = curtains.

sentences.t: Rothkäppchen aber war nach den Blumen herum gelaufen, und als es so viel zusammen hatte, daß es keine mehr tragen konnte, fiel ihm die Großmutter wieder ein und es machte sich auf den Weg zu ihr.
sentences.l: Little Red Cap, however, had run about after the flowers, and when it had so many together that it could carry no more, the grandmother came to its mind again and it set out on the way to her.
sentences.i: But Little Red Cap had been running about after the flowers, and when she had gathered so many that she could carry no more, she remembered Grandmother again and set out on her way to her.
sentences.n: “herum gelaufen” (herumlaufen, ‘to run about’) is separable. “fiel ihm ... ein” — einfallen + dative = ‘to occur to, come to mind’ (Grandmother occurred to her). “sich auf den Weg machen” = to set out.

sentences.t: Es wunderte sich daß die Thüre aufstand, und wie es in die Stube trat, so kam es ihm so seltsam darin vor, daß es dachte „ei, du mein Gott, wie ängstlich wird mirs heute zu Muth, und bin sonst so gerne bei der Großmutter!“
sentences.l: It wondered that the door stood open, and as it stepped into the room, it seemed to it so strange in there, that it thought, “oh, my goodness, how anxious I feel today, and am otherwise so glad to be with grandmother!”
sentences.i: She was surprised that the door stood open, and as she stepped into the room, it seemed so strange to her in there that she thought, “Oh, good heavens, how uneasy I feel today — and I usually so love being at Grandmother’s!”
sentences.n: “sich wundern” = to be surprised. “die Thüre stand auf” — aufstehen here, of a door, means ‘to stand open.’ “kam ... vor” — vorkommen + dative = ‘to seem, appear (to someone).’ “wie wird mirs zu Muth” is an idiom, ‘how I feel (in my heart)’; “Muth” is the 1857 spelling of Mut, “mirs” = mir es.

sentences.t: Es rief „guten Morgen,“ bekam aber keine Antwort.
sentences.l: It called “good morning,” but got no answer.
sentences.i: She called “good morning,” but received no answer.

sentences.t: Darauf gieng es zum Bett und zog die Vorhänge zurück: da lag die Großmutter, und hatte die Haube tief ins Gesicht gesetzt und sah so wunderlich aus.
sentences.l: Thereupon it went to the bed and drew the curtains back: there lay the grandmother, and had set the bonnet deep into her face and looked so peculiar.
sentences.i: Then she went to the bed and drew back the curtains: there lay Grandmother, with her cap pulled low over her face, and looking so strange.
sentences.n: “zog ... zurück” (zurückziehen) and “sah ... aus” (aussehen, ‘to look, appear’) are separable. “wunderlich” = odd, peculiar.

sentences.t: „Ei, Großmutter, was hast du für große Ohren!“
sentences.l: “Oh, Grandmother, what big ears you have!”
sentences.n: “was hast du für große Ohren” — the “was ... für” construction expresses ‘what (a) kind of,’ here in an exclamation. These four parallel exchanges are the famous heart of the tale.

sentences.t: „Daß ich dich besser hören kann.“
sentences.l: “So that I can hear you the better.”
sentences.i: “The better to hear you with.”
sentences.n: “Daß” (1857 for dass) here opens a clause of purpose, ‘so that.’ The reply is elliptical — ‘[I have them] so that I can hear you better.’

sentences.t: „Ei, Großmutter, was hast du für große Augen!“
sentences.l: “Oh, Grandmother, what big eyes you have!”

sentences.t: „Daß ich dich besser sehen kann.“
sentences.l: “So that I can see you the better.”
sentences.i: “The better to see you with.”

sentences.t: „Ei, Großmutter, was hast du für große Hände!“
sentences.l: “Oh, Grandmother, what big hands you have!”

sentences.t: „Daß ich dich besser packen kann.“
sentences.l: “So that I can seize you the better.”
sentences.i: “The better to grab you with.”
sentences.n: “packen” = to seize, grab hold of.

sentences.t: „Aber, Großmutter, was hast du für ein entsetzlich großes Maul!“
sentences.l: “But, Grandmother, what a dreadfully big mouth you have!”
sentences.n: “Maul” is the mouth or muzzle of an animal — a blunt word, fitting now that the wolf is all but unmasked. “entsetzlich” = dreadfully, terribly.

sentences.t: „Daß ich dich besser fressen kann.“
sentences.l: “So that I can eat you the better.”
sentences.i: “The better to eat you with.”
sentences.n: “fressen” is ‘to eat’ said of animals (to devour), as opposed to essen for people — here chillingly of the wolf.

sentences.t: Kaum hatte der Wolf das gesagt, so that er einen Satz aus dem Bette und verschlang das arme Rothkäppchen.
sentences.l: Scarcely had the wolf said that, then he made a leap out of the bed and devoured the poor Little Red Cap.
sentences.i: Scarcely had the wolf said this than he made one leap out of the bed and swallowed poor Little Red Cap.
sentences.n: “Kaum ... so” = ‘scarcely ... when.’ “einen Satz thun” — Satz here means ‘a leap, bound’; “that” is the 1857 spelling of tat. “verschlang” (verschlingen) = to devour, gulp down. “Bette” has the old dative -e.

sentences.t: Wie der Wolf sein Gelüsten gestillt hatte, legte er sich wieder ins Bett, schlief ein und fieng an überlaut zu schnarchen.
sentences.l: When the wolf had stilled his craving, he laid himself back into the bed, fell asleep and began to snore over-loud.
sentences.i: When the wolf had satisfied his greed, he lay down again in the bed, fell asleep, and began to snore very loudly.
sentences.n: “Wie” = ‘when.’ “sein Gelüsten stillen” = to satisfy one’s craving. “schlief ein” (einschlafen, ‘to fall asleep’) and “fieng an” (anfangen, ‘to begin’; 1857 for fing an) are separable. “überlaut” = exceedingly loud.

sentences.t: Der Jäger gieng eben an dem Haus vorbei und dachte „wie die alte Frau schnarcht, du mußt doch sehen ob ihr etwas fehlt.“
sentences.l: The huntsman was just going past the house and thought, “how the old woman snores; you must just see whether anything ails her.”
sentences.i: The huntsman happened to be passing the house just then and thought, “How the old woman snores — I had better see whether anything is wrong with her.”
sentences.n: “gieng ... vorbei” (vorbeigehen, ‘to go past’) is separable; “eben” = just (then). “ob ihr etwas fehlt” — fehlen + dative, ‘whether something is lacking to her,’ i.e. ‘whether anything is wrong.’ “doch” is a nudging particle, ‘just, do.’

sentences.t: Da trat er in die Stube, und wie er vor das Bette kam, so sah er daß der Wolf darin lag.
sentences.l: So he stepped into the room, and as he came before the bed, he saw that the wolf lay in it.
sentences.i: So he stepped into the room, and when he came up to the bed, he saw that the wolf was lying in it.
sentences.n: “trat” is the preterite of treten (‘to step’). “darin” = ‘in it, therein.’

sentences.t: „Finde ich dich hier, du alter Sünder,“ sagte er, „ich habe dich lange gesucht.“
sentences.l: “Do I find you here, you old sinner,” said he, “I have long sought you.”
sentences.i: “So I find you here, you old sinner,” he said; “I have been looking for you a long time.”
sentences.n: “Finde ich dich hier” — the verb-first form here is exclamatory, ‘so I find you here!’ “gesucht” is the past participle of suchen (‘to seek, look for’).

sentences.t: Nun wollte er seine Büchse anlegen, da fiel ihm ein der Wolf könnte die Großmutter gefressen haben, und sie wäre noch zu retten: schoß nicht, sondern nahm eine Scheere und fieng an dem schlafenden Wolf den Bauch aufzuschneiden.
sentences.l: Now he wanted to level his gun, when it occurred to him the wolf might have eaten the grandmother, and she might yet be to save: he shot not, but took a pair of scissors and began to cut open the belly of the sleeping wolf.
sentences.i: He was about to take aim with his gun when it struck him that the wolf might have eaten the grandmother, and that she might yet be saved: so he did not shoot, but took a pair of scissors and began to cut open the sleeping wolf’s belly.
sentences.n: “die Büchse anlegen” = to level / take aim with a gun (separable an-). “fiel ihm ein” (einfallen + dative) = ‘it occurred to him.’ “könnte ... gefressen haben” and “wäre ... zu retten” are subjunctive II of possibility. “Scheere” is the 1857 spelling of Schere (‘scissors’). “fieng an ... aufzuschneiden” — anfangen with a zu-infinitive of the separable aufschneiden (‘to cut open’).

sentences.t: Wie er ein paar Schnitte gethan hatte, da sah er das rothe Käppchen leuchten, und noch ein paar Schnitte, da sprang das Mädchen heraus und rief „ach, wie war ich erschrocken, wie wars so dunkel in dem Wolf seinem Leib!“
sentences.l: When he had done a couple of cuts, then he saw the red cap gleam, and a couple more cuts, then the girl sprang out and cried, “oh, how frightened I was, how dark it was in the wolf’s body!”
sentences.i: When he had made a few cuts, he saw the red cap gleaming, and after a few more cuts the girl sprang out and cried, “Oh, how frightened I was — how dark it was inside the wolf’s body!”
sentences.n: “Schnitte gethan” — einen Schnitt thun = to make a cut; “gethan” is the 1857 spelling of getan. “erschrocken” is the participle of erschrecken (‘to be frightened, startled’). “in dem Wolf seinem Leib” is a colloquial possessive (‘the wolf his body’ = the wolf’s body), common in speech; “Leib” = body.

sentences.t: Und dann kam die alte Großmutter auch noch lebendig heraus und konnte kaum athmen.
sentences.l: And then the old grandmother also came out still alive and could scarcely breathe.
sentences.i: And then the old grandmother, too, came out still alive, and could hardly breathe.
sentences.n: “kam ... heraus” (herauskommen) is separable. “lebendig” = alive. “athmen” is the 1857 spelling of atmen (‘to breathe’), with the old -th-.

sentences.t: Rothkäppchen aber holte geschwind große Steine, damit füllten sie dem Wolf den Leib, und wie er aufwachte, wollte er fortspringen, aber die Steine waren so schwer, daß er gleich niedersank und sich todt fiel.
sentences.l: Little Red Cap, however, quickly fetched big stones; with them they filled the wolf’s belly, and when he awoke, he wanted to spring away, but the stones were so heavy that he at once sank down and fell himself dead.
sentences.i: But Little Red Cap quickly fetched big stones, and they filled the wolf’s belly with them; and when he woke and tried to spring away, the stones were so heavy that he sank down at once and fell dead.
sentences.n: “dem Wolf den Leib füllen” — a dative of the possessor (‘filled the belly of the wolf’). “aufwachte” (aufwachen), “fortspringen” (‘to spring away’) and “niedersank” (niedersinken) are all separable. “sich todt fallen” = to fall down dead; “todt” is the 1857 spelling of tot.

sentences.t: Da waren alle drei vergnügt; der Jäger zog dem Wolf den Pelz ab und gieng damit heim, die Großmutter aß den Kuchen und trank den Wein den Rothkäppchen gebracht hatte, und erholte sich wieder, Rothkäppchen aber dachte „du willst dein Lebtag nicht wieder allein vom Wege ab in den Wald laufen, wenn dirs die Mutter verboten hat.“
sentences.l: Then all three were content; the huntsman drew the wolf’s fur off and went home with it, the grandmother ate the cake and drank the wine that Little Red Cap had brought, and recovered herself again, but Little Red Cap thought, “you will never again in your life run off the path into the wood alone, when your mother has forbidden it you.”
sentences.i: Then all three were content: the huntsman skinned the wolf and went home with the pelt; the grandmother ate the cake and drank the wine that Little Red Cap had brought, and recovered; and Little Red Cap thought to herself, “never again as long as you live will you run off the path into the forest alone, when your mother has forbidden it.”
sentences.n: “zog ... ab” — dem Wolf den Pelz abziehen = to strip off the wolf’s pelt (abziehen separable, with dative of the possessor). “erholte sich” (sich erholen) = recovered. “dein Lebtag” is an old adverbial accusative, ‘all your life long.’ “dirs” = dir es; “verboten” is the past participle of verbieten (‘to forbid’).

sentences.t: Es wird auch erzählt, daß einmal, als Rothkäppchen der alten Großmutter wieder Gebackenes brachte, ein anderer Wolf ihm zugesprochen und es vom Wege habe ableiten wollen.
sentences.l: It is also told that once, when Little Red Cap was again bringing baked goods to the old grandmother, another wolf spoke to it and wanted to lead it off the path.
sentences.i: It is also told that once, when Little Red Cap was again taking baked things to the old grandmother, another wolf spoke to her and tried to lead her off the path.
sentences.n: “Es wird ... erzählt” = ‘it is told’ (passive). “Gebackenes” is a neuter noun from the participle of backen, ‘something baked, baked goods.’ In the reported clause the verbs stand in subjunctive I: “habe ... ableiten wollen” (‘tried to lead away’) — the reported-speech subjunctive that runs all through Grimm. “zugesprochen” (zusprechen + dative) = ‘spoke to, addressed’; “ableiten” (‘to lead away, divert’) is separable.

sentences.t: Rothkäppchen aber hütete sich und gieng gerade fort seines Wegs und sagte der Großmutter daß es dem Wolf begegnet wäre, der ihm guten Tag gewünscht, aber so bös aus den Augen geguckt hätte: „wenns nicht auf offner Straße gewesen wäre, er hätte mich gefressen.“
sentences.l: Little Red Cap, however, was on her guard and went straight on her way and told the grandmother that it had met the wolf, who had wished it good day, but had looked so wickedly out of his eyes: “had it not been on the open road, he would have eaten me.”
sentences.i: But Little Red Cap was on her guard, and went straight on her way, and told the grandmother that she had met the wolf, who had wished her good day but had had such a wicked look in his eyes: “if it had not been on the open road, he would have eaten me.”
sentences.n: “sich hüten” = to be on one’s guard, take care. “seines Wegs gehen” — a genitive, ‘to go one’s way.’ The reported speech is all subjunctive: “begegnet wäre ... gewünscht [hätte] ... geguckt hätte.” “bös aus den Augen gucken” = ‘to look wickedly out of one’s eyes,’ i.e. to have an evil look; “bös” is a short form of böse, “geguckt” from gucken. “wenns” = wenn es; “offner” = offener (‘open’).

sentences.t: „Komm,“ sagte die Großmutter, „wir wollen die Thüre verschließen, daß er nicht herein kann.“
sentences.l: “Come,” said the grandmother, “we will lock the door, so that he cannot get in.”
sentences.n: “wir wollen ... verschließen” — wollen here forms a soft ‘let us.’ “verschließen” = to lock, shut fast. “daß ... nicht herein kann” — the verb of motion is dropped after the modal (‘cannot [come] in’).

sentences.t: Bald darnach klopfte der Wolf an und rief „mach auf, Großmutter, ich bin das Rothkäppchen, ich bring dir Gebackenes.“
sentences.l: Soon after that the wolf knocked and cried, “open up, Grandmother, I am Little Red Cap, I bring you baked things.”
sentences.n: “Bald darnach” = soon after that; “darnach” is the 1857 spelling of danach. “klopfte an” (anklopfen) and “mach auf” (aufmachen) are separable. The wolf pretends to be the child.

sentences.t: Sie schwiegen aber still und machten die Thüre nicht auf: da schlich der Graukopf etlichemal um das Haus, sprang endlich aufs Dach und wollte warten bis Rothkäppchen Abends nach Haus gienge, dann wollte er ihm nachschleichen und wollts in der Dunkelheit fressen.
sentences.l: But they kept quite silent and did not open the door: then the grey-head crept several times around the house, sprang at last onto the roof and wanted to wait until Little Red Cap should go home in the evening, then he wanted to sneak after it and eat it in the darkness.
sentences.i: But they kept quite silent and did not open the door: then the grey-head slunk several times around the house, sprang at last onto the roof, and meant to wait until Little Red Cap should go home in the evening, and then to creep after her and eat her in the dark.
sentences.n: “schwiegen still” — schweigen (‘to keep silent’), preterite plural. “Graukopf” (‘grey-head’) is a nickname for the wolf. “etlichemal” = several times (archaic). “bis ... gienge” — the subjunctive gienge (1857 for ginge) after bis, of an anticipated action. “nachschleichen” (+ dative, ‘to creep after’) is separable; “wollts” = wollte es.

sentences.t: Aber die Großmutter merkte was er im Sinn hatte.
sentences.l: But the grandmother noticed what he had in mind.
sentences.n: “etwas im Sinn haben” = to have something in mind, to be up to something.

sentences.t: Nun stand vor dem Haus ein großer Steintrog, da sprach sie zu dem Kind „nimm den Eimer, Rothkäppchen, gestern hab ich Würste gekocht, da trag das Wasser, worin sie gekocht sind, in den Trog.“
sentences.l: Now there stood before the house a great stone trough; so she spoke to the child, “take the bucket, Little Red Cap, yesterday I cooked sausages, so carry the water in which they were cooked into the trough.”
sentences.i: Now before the house there stood a great stone trough, and she said to the child, “Take the bucket, Little Red Cap; yesterday I boiled sausages — carry the water they were boiled in into the trough.”
sentences.n: “Steintrog” (Stein ‘stone’ + Trog). “Eimer” = bucket, pail. “worin” = ‘wherein, in which.’ “Würste” is the plural of Wurst (‘sausages’). “gekocht sind” — an older perfect/passive, ‘were cooked / have been cooked.’

sentences.t: Rothkäppchen trug so lange, bis der große große Trog ganz voll war.
sentences.l: Little Red Cap carried so long, until the great great trough was quite full.
sentences.i: Little Red Cap carried until the great, great trough was quite full.
sentences.n: “so lange, bis” = ‘so long, until.’ The doubled “große große” intensifies — the trough is enormous.

sentences.t: Da stieg der Geruch von den Würsten dem Wolf in die Nase, er schnupperte und guckte hinab, endlich machte er den Hals so lang, daß er sich nicht mehr halten konnte, und anfieng zu rutschen: so rutschte er vom Dach herab, gerade in den großen Trog hinein und ertrank.
sentences.l: Then the smell of the sausages rose into the wolf’s nose; he sniffed and peered down, at last made his neck so long that he could no longer hold himself, and began to slide: so he slid down from the roof, straight into the great trough, and drowned.
sentences.i: Then the smell of the sausages rose to the wolf’s nose; he sniffed and peered down, and at last stretched his neck so far that he could no longer keep his balance, and began to slide: and so he slid down from the roof, straight into the great trough, and drowned.
sentences.n: “stieg ... dem Wolf in die Nase” — a dative of the possessor (‘into the wolf’s nose’). “schnupperte” (schnuppern, ‘to sniff’) and “guckte hinab” (‘peered down’). “sich halten” = to hold / steady oneself. “anfieng” (anfangen; 1857 for anfing) and “rutschte ... herab / hinein” — rutschen (‘to slide, slip’). “ertrank” is the preterite of ertrinken (‘to drown’).

sentences.t: Rothkäppchen aber gieng fröhlich nach Haus, und that ihm niemand etwas zu Leid.
sentences.l: Little Red Cap, however, went merrily home, and no one did it any harm.
sentences.i: But Little Red Cap went home merrily, and no one did her any harm.
sentences.n: “that” is the 1857 spelling of tat (thun = tun). “jemandem etwas zu Leid thun” is an idiom, ‘to do someone harm, to hurt someone’ — here negated, a closing reassurance to match the fairy-tale ending.
